questionText: State the exact period of $y = \sec x$ and of $y = \operatorname{cosec} x$, and explain briefly why they share this period.

steps.explanation: Secant and cosecant are built directly from cosine and sine by taking reciprocals, so their repeating behaviour is inherited from $\cos x$ and $\sin x$.

steps.description: Recall the period of the parent functions
steps.explanation: Both $\cos x$ and $\sin x$ repeat every $360^\circ$ (that is $2\pi$ radians); this is the smallest shift that returns every value, so their period is $360^\circ$.
Replacing $x$ with $x+360^\circ$ leaves the cosine unchanged, so its reciprocal is unchanged too. Secant therefore repeats after $360^\circ$.
The same reasoning works for cosecant: since sine is unchanged by a $360^\circ$ shift, so is its reciprocal.

A shift of $180^\circ$ does not return cosine to the same value — here it turns $1$ into $-1$ — so no interval shorter than $360^\circ$ makes sec or cosec repeat. Hence $360^\circ$ is genuinely the period.

steps.description: Give the period in both units
steps.explanation: Expressing the period in degrees and radians makes it usable in either convention.

steps.workingLatex: y = \sec x \text{ and } y = \operatorname{cosec} x \text{ both have period } 360^\circ
steps.explanation: Both graphs are copies of one $360^\circ$-wide block repeated forever, which is exactly what having period $360^\circ$ means.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: Both $y=\sec x$ and $y=\operatorname{cosec} x$ have period $360^\circ$ (that is $2\pi$ radians), inherited from the $360^\circ$ period of $\cos x$ and $\sin x$.
